Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Given event log, we need to compute durations. Lets parse cases. Case 2001: timestamps from 2024-04-01 09:00 to 2024-04-01 10:30 (Close Claim). Duration: start 09:00, end 10:30: 1.5 hours (90 minutes). Thats relatively short. Case 2002: timestamps from 2024-04-01 09:05 (Submit) to 2024-04-02 11:00 (Close). Duration: 1 day + 1h55? Lets compute: from 09:05 on 04-01 to 09:05 on 04-02 is 24 hours, plus from 09:05 to 11:00 = 1h55. So total 25h55. 25.92 hours. Case 2003: from 2024-04-01 09:10 (Submit) to 2024-04-03 09:30 (Close). Thats 2 days + 0h20? Lets compute: 09:10 to 09:10 on 04-03 = 48 hours, then add 20 minutes: total 48h20m. So about 48.33 hours. Case 2004: from 2024-04-01 09:20 to 2024-04-01 10:45: 1h25 min. Case 2005: from 2024-04-01 09:25 to 2024-04-04 14:30: Thats 3 days, 5h5min? Lets compute: from 04-01 09:25 to 04-04 09:25 = 72 hours (3 days). Then from 09:25 to 14:30 = 5h5min. So total 77h5min (approx 3 days, 5 hours).

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Thus cases with performance issues: 2002, 2003, 2005 (especially 2005). Also possibly 2002 (though it is only 1 day). The significantly longer can be considered relative to average. Cases 2001 and 2004 have short durations (2 hours). Cases 2002 26 hours, 2003 48 hours, 2005 77 hours.

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Now analyze attributes. Resource: For each case, resources are used. Cases with long durations involve Adjuster_Lisa (2002, 2004? Actually 2004 uses Adjuster_Lisa for Evaluate only; but 2002 uses Adjuster_Lisa for Evaluate and Request Additional Documents; 2005 uses Adjuster_Lisa for Evaluate and three Request Additional Documents events). Cases with long durations also involve Manager_Bill (for Approve) for 2003 and 2005.

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Case 2002: steps: Submit (CSR_Paul) 09:05, Evaluate (Adjuster_Lisa) 09:45 (40 min later). Then Request Additional Documents (Adjuster_Lisa) 14:00 (4h15 later). Then Approve (Manager_Ann) 10:00 next day (20h later). So a big gap between Request Additional Documents and Approve (approx 20h). The request probably waiting for response. Also maybe due to resource availability (Adjuster_Lisa). But there is also a gap between Submit and Evaluate (40 min). Not huge. The major delay between Request Additional Documents and Approve suggests waiting for the claimant to provide docs. But the request was at 14:00 on 04-01 and Approve at 10:00 on 04-02. Thats about 20 hours; maybe the claim needed documents and the delay was due to Region B? The region is B.

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Case 2003: Complexity High. Steps: Submit 09:10, Evaluate 09:40 (30 min). Then Request Additional Documents (Adjuster_Mike) at 11:00 (80 min after Evaluate). Then another Request Additional Documents (Adjuster_Mike) at 17:00 (6 hours later). Approve at 04-02 16:00 (23 hours after second request). Then Pay 04-03 09:00 (17 hours after Approve). So a lot of waiting time between requests, and then a long waiting period before approval (maybe waiting for docs).

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Case 2005: Complexity High. Steps: Submit 09:25, Evaluate 09:50 (25 min). Request Additional Documents (Adjuster_Lisa) at 11:30 (1h40 after Evaluate). Then second Request Additional Documents at 04-02 17:00 (30.5 hours later). Then third Request Additional Documents at 04-03 15:00 (22h later). Approve 04-04 10:00 (19h after third request). So huge delays between requests. Also region B. So region B might cause delays. Also Adjuster_Lisa handling many requests; perhaps overloaded.

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Thus root causes likely: Complexity: High complexity leads to multiple requests for docs, extended waiting. Cases 2003 and 2005 are High; they took longer. Resource: Adjuster_Lisa appears in both cases with high complexity and region B. She performed multiple requests. Possibly overloaded or slower. Manager_Bill appears for high complexity cases, also maybe overloaded. Region: Cases from Region B (2002, 2004, 2005) have longer durations for high complexity (2005) and medium complexity (2002). But 2004 is also region B but short (low complexity).

Name: Deductive Reasoning - IND
Text: Compute average duration: (90 + 1555 + 2900 + 85 + 4625) min = total 9255 min. Divided by 5 = 1851 min = 30.85h. standard deviation? ... outlier threshold: mean + 1.5*std 30.85h + 1.5*29h = 30.85 + 43.5 = 74.35h. Cases 74.35h: 2005 (77.08h) is above threshold. Also maybe 2003 (48.33h) is not above.

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Complexity: High complexity leads to more doc requests, longer durations. Cases 2003 (high) took 48h, 2005 (high) took 77h. Medium complexity (2002) took 26h. Low complexity (2001,2004) short (2h). So complexity is a factor. Resource: Adjuster_Lisa appears in cases 2002 (medium) and 2005 (high) with multiple doc requests. Region: Cases 2002 and 2005 are region B, but region A also has high complexity case 2003 with similar patterns. So region may amplify effect of complexity, but not primary cause. Thus root cause: High complexity claims require multiple rounds of doc requests, which are not handled promptly due to limited resource capacity (adjuster overloaded), and region B may have less capacity.

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: Mitigations: 1. Resource capacity: Hire additional adjusters, especially for high complexity. Cross-train adjusters to handle doc requests faster. Set SLA for doc request response. 2. Process redesign: For high complexity claims, perform a preliminary document checklist at submission to reduce later requests. 3. Monitoring escalation: Track number of doc requests per claim; after 2 requests, escalate to senior adjuster or manager. 4. Region-specific actions: Region B may need more staffing. 5. Complexity handling: Classify high complexity claims early and allocate more resources. 6. Use technology: Provide digital upload portal to speed up doc collection.
